title: Governments are still underestimating the power of sports
gist: Former French deputy sports minister Rama Yade argues governments systematically underuse sports as a policy lever, and should treat the sports ecosystem as an investable asset class—alongside infrastructure, tech, and real estate—to attract institutional capital, while guarding against national-security risks from foreign ownership.

insights.claim: Governments should introduce policies that develop sports as an asset class to attract institutional capital, treating it alongside infrastructure, technology, and real estate.
insights.speaker: Rama Yade
insights.so_what: Framing sports as an institutional asset class signals a growing allocation channel for sponsorship, broadcasting, and franchise investment.
insights.quote: governments should introduce policies that harness the sports ecosystem to attract institutional capital and develop sports as an asset class, alongside infrastructure, technology, and real estate

insights.claim: Major sporting events stimulate serious investment because mass fan bases require stadiums, transportation, and housing, while generating sponsorship and broadcasting-rights revenue.
insights.speaker: Rama Yade
insights.so_what: Maps the concrete investment multiplier from hosting events—relevant to infrastructure and hospitality plays around tournaments.
insights.quote: Sports franchises, therefore, stimulate serious investment for the countries hosting them.
insights.locator: As I witnessed at the France-Senegal game

insights.claim: Foreign ownership of sports assets raises national-security implications requiring policy intervention, with standards needed to ensure transparency, prevent corruption, and protect ESG principles.
insights.speaker: Rama Yade
insights.so_what: Foreign-ownership scrutiny in sports could create regulatory friction for cross-border franchise deals—watch for screening regimes.
insights.quote: National security implications may arise from foreign ownership of sports assets, requiring policy interventions.
insights.locator: With this, however, governments should ensure

insights.claim: Sports can sharply escalate geopolitical tension, as when 1969 World Cup qualifiers exacerbated El Salvador-Honduras tensions into a four-day war killing up to 1,500.
insights.speaker: Rama Yade
insights.so_what: Illustrates sports as a double-edged geopolitical force, not merely soft power—relevant to event-related instability risk.
insights.quote: led to a four-day armed war that, according to official estimates, killed up to 1,500 people

insights.claim: US Title IX expanded high-school girls' soccer from 700 players in 1972 to nearly 400,000 today, illustrating how targeted sports policy unlocks talent and economic activity.
insights.speaker: Rama Yade
insights.so_what: Quantifies the long-run economic payoff of sports policy; a template for participation-driven market creation.
insights.quote: In 1972, only seven hundred girls played high school soccer across the country. But today, nearly 400,000 girls play.
insights.locator: Women's soccer in the United States demonstrates

insights.claim: The 14 teams of the National Women's Soccer League generated $262 million in revenue last year, showing sports-policy interventions translate into measurable commercial scale.
insights.speaker: Rama Yade
insights.so_what: Concrete revenue figure validating women's-sports as a growing investable market segment.
insights.quote: The fourteen teams across the National Women's Soccer League, for example, generated $262 million in revenue last year.
